test_text.title: Making an Insurance Claim for Damaged Shipment

audioscript.text: Section 1, you will hear a conversation between a representative of an insurance company and a customer.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning. Total Insurance. Judy speaking.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: How may I help you?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: I recently shipped my belongings from overseas back here to Australia, and I took out insurance with your company.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Uh. Some items were damaged during the move, so I need to make a claim.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: What do I have to do?
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: OK, well, first I need to get a few details about this.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Can you give me your name, please?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Michael Alexander.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: The man's name is Michael Alexander.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So Michael Alexander has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning. Total Insurance. Judy speaking.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: How may I help you?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: I recently shipped my belongings from overseas back here to Australia, and I took out insurance with your company.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Uh. Some items were damaged during the move, so I need to make a claim.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: What do I have to do?
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: OK, well, first I need to get a few details about this.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Can you give me your name, please?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Michael Alexander.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: OK, and your address please?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: My old address or my current one?
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Your current one.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: It's 24 Manly Street.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Milperra near Sydney.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: What was the suburb, sorry?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Milperra. MILPERRA.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Now, who was the shipping agent, Mr Alexander?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Hmm. You mean the company we used?
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Yes, the company who packed everything up at the point of origin.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Oh, it was... er... First Class Movers.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: OK, uh, where were the goods shipped from?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: China, but the ship came via Singapore, and was there for about a week.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Don't worry, all of that information will be in the documentation.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Now the dates, do you know when the ship arrived?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: It left on the 11th of October and got to Sydney on the 28th of November.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: OK, I need one more thing.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: There's a reference number.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: It should be in the top right-hand corner of the pink form they gave you.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Uh. Let me have a look.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: I have so many papers.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, here it is.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: It's 601ACK.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 4 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 4 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I need to take down a few details of the actual damage over the phone before you put in a full report.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Can you tell me how many items were damaged, and what the damage was?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Yes, well, four things actually.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: I'll start with the big things.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: My TV first of all, it's a large one, very expensive.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Our Insurance doesn't cover electrical problems.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: It isn't an electrical problem.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: The screen has a huge crack in it, so it's unusable.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Any idea of the price to repair it?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Well, I don't think it can be repaired.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: It will need a new one.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: OK, I'll make a note of that, and we'll see what we can do.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Now, what was the second item?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: The cabinet from the bathroom was damaged as well.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: It's a lovely cabinet, we use it to keep our towels in.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: And what is the extent of the damage?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Well, the back and the sides seem OK, but the door has a huge hole in it.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: It can't be repaired.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: I'm really not very happy about it.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: And how much do you think it will cost to replace it?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Well, when I bought it last year, I paid $125 for it.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: But the one I've seen here in Sydney is a bit more expensive, it's $140.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Right, and what was the third item?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: My dining room table, it's a lovely table from Indonesia.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: It must have been very hot inside the container, because one leg has completely split down the middle.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: The top and the other 3 look OK, thank goodness.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: Any idea of the price to repair it?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Well, I had an estimate done on this actually, because it is a very special table to us.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: They quoted us $200 which is really pricey.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: So I hope the insurance will cover the total cost.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: I'm sure that will be fine.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Uh. What was the last item, Mr Alexander?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Well, we have a lovely set of china plates and dishes, you know, with matching cups, saucers, the lot.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: They were all in the one box, which must have got dropped, because some plates were broken, 6 actually.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: And can you tell me the replacement value of these?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Well, it's hard to say, because they were part of a set, but they can be up to $10 each, as it's such a good set.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: OK, so that would be around $60 altogether.
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: And is that all of the items?
audioscript.speaker: JUDY

audioscript.text: Yes, so what do I have to do now?
audioscript.speaker: MICHAEL

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 2, you will hear an introductory talk about a new agricultural park.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Welcome to Green Vale Agricultural Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you know, we've only been open a week, so you're amongst our first visitors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have lots of fascinating indoor and outdoor exhibits on our huge complex spreading hundreds of hectares.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our remit is to give educational opportunities to the wider public, as well as to offer research sites for a wide variety of agriculturists and other scientists.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let's start by seeing what there is to do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you can see, uh, here on our giant wall plan, we are now situated in the Reception block.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Here.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As you walk out of the main door into the park, there's a path you can follow.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you follow this route, you'll immediately come into the Rare Breeds section.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Where we keep a wide variety of animals, which I shall be telling you a little more about later.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Next to this, uh, moving east is the large grazing area for the rare breeds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then further east in the largest section of our park is the Forest Area. South of the grazing area, and in fact just next to the Reception block is our experimental crop area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the middle of the park, uh, this circular area is our lake.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And these two small rectangular shapes here are the fish farms where we rear fish for sale.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To the east of those is the marsh area, which attracts a great many migrant birds, and in the southeastern corner beyond the marsh is our market garden area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Growing vegetables and flowers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: All these areas can be visited by the general public for almost all the year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Although please take note of the large signs at the entrance to each area, which tell which tell you when certain areas are being used for particular controlled experiments.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And are therefore temporarily out of bounds to the public.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can see for yourself what a huge area the park covers, and a key question is always, how can we move around?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, you have a choice of means.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All environmentally friendly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Um. Cars are banned in the park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have bicycles, which you can hire behind the Reception block... here...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: the healthy ones of you can go on foot,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: and finally there's our electric tram, powered from solar cells.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You find more information about this at the front entrance.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A good place to start on your tour is the Rare Breeds section.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We keep goats, sheep, and hens, and other kinds of poultry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. We're also thinking of bringing in cows and horses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we do not as yet have facilities for these bigger animals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The animals are fed in public twice a day, and a short lecture given on their feeding habits and nutritional needs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These are very popular with the public, but of course, we mustn't lose sight of the main purpose of having this section, not as such to preserve rare animals, but to maintain the diversity of breeds, to broaden the gene pool for agricultural development.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Green Vale changes with the seasons with different events happening at different times of the year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: May will be perhaps our most spectacular month with the arrival of the Canada geese.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And when our fruit trees will be in full blossom, but there are interesting events all year round... for example, John Havers, our expert fly fisherman, is currently giving displays on the lake.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Each of the sections has its own seasonal calendar, and please consult the summary board at the main entrance. And the final section, as we return to the Reception block, is the orchard.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Do take time to browse round our shop, there's a wide selection of books on wildlife, some of them written by local authors, and the history of farming.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Including organic farming, something which the park will be diversifying into in the coming months.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Then further east in the largest section of our park is the Forest Area. South of the grazing area, and in fact just next to the Reception block is our experimental crop area.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the middle of the park, uh, this circular area is our lake.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And these two small rectangular shapes here are the fish farms where we rear fish for sale.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To the east of those is the marsh area, which attracts a great many migrant birds, and in the southeastern corner beyond the marsh is our market garden area.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Growing vegetables and flowers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: All these areas can be visited by the general public for almost all the year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Although please take note of the large signs at the entrance to each area, which tell which tell you when certain areas are being used for particular controlled experiments.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And are therefore temporarily out of bounds to the public.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You can see for yourself what a huge area the park covers, and a key question is always, how can we move around?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, you have a choice of means.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All environmentally friendly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Um. Cars are banned in the park.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We have bicycles, which you can hire behind the Reception block... here...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: the healthy ones of you can go on foot,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: and finally there's our electric tram, powered from solar cells.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You find more information about this at the front entrance.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A good place to start on your tour is the Rare Breeds section.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We keep goats, sheep, and hens, and other kinds of poultry.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. We're also thinking of bringing in cows and horses.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But we do not as yet have facilities for these bigger animals.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The animals are fed in public twice a day, and a short lecture given on their feeding habits and nutritional needs.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These are very popular with the public, but of course, we mustn't lose sight of the main purpose of having this section, not as such to preserve rare animals, but to maintain the diversity of breeds, to broaden the gene pool for agricultural development.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Green Vale changes with the seasons with different events happening at different times of the year.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: May will be perhaps our most spectacular month with the arrival of the Canada geese.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And when our fruit trees will be in full blossom, but there are interesting events all year round... for example, John Havers, our expert fly fisherman, is currently giving displays on the lake.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Each of the sections has its own seasonal calendar, and please consult the summary board at the main entrance. And the final section, as we return to the Reception block, is the orchard.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Do take time to browse round our shop, there's a wide selection of books on wildlife, some of them written by local authors, and the history of farming.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Including organic farming, something which the park will be diversifying into in the coming months.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: According to the speaker, the main purposes of the park are
When are the experimental areas closed to the public?
How can you move around the park?
The rare breed animals kept in the park include
What is the main purpose of having the Rare Breeds Section?
options: to keep a variety of breeds

items.title: What can you see in the park at the present time?

test_text.title: Research on Preventing the Introduction of Asian Honey Bees to Australia

audioscript.text: Section 3, you will hear part of a seminar in which a researcher called Grant Freeman discusses his work on Australian honeybees.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: With a group of students.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning everyone.
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: Uh. In today's seminar, Grant Freeman, a biologist who specializes in identifying insects, and who works for the Australian Quarantine Service, has come to talk to us about his current research work.
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: Well, uh, over to you, Grant.
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: Good morning, everyone.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: I'm sure that you know that the quarantine service regulates all food brought into Australia.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Well, obviously they want to protect Australia from diseases that might come in with imported goods, but they also want to prevent insect pests from being introduced into the country.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: And that's where I have a part to play.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Anyway, my current research involves trying to find a particular type of bee, the Asian Honey Bee.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: And finding out whether there are any of them around in various states of Australia.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: We discovered a few of them in Queensland once and eradicated them.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Now we're pretty keen to make sure that there aren't any more getting in, particularly to New South Wales and other states.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: What's wrong with Asian Honey Bees?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Are they so different from Australian bees?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Well, in fact, they look almost the same, but they are infested with mites, microscopic creatures which live on them, and which can seriously damage our own homegrown bees, or could even wipe them out.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Well, what would happen if Australian bees died out?
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: Well, the honey from Australian bees is of excellent quality, much better than the stuff the Asian bees produce.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: In fact Australia exports native queen bees to a large number of countries because of this.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: When the European Honey Bee was first discovered out in the bush, we found they made really unpleasant honey, and they were also too big to pollinate many of our native flowers here in Australia.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: That must have had a devastating effect on the natural flora.
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: Did you lose any species?
audioscript.speaker: STUDENT

audioscript.text: No, we managed to get them under control before that happened, but if Asian bees got in, there could be other consequences.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: We could lose a lot of money, because you might not be aware, but it's estimated that native bees' pollination of flower and vegetable crops is worth $1.2 billion a year.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: So in a way, they're the farmers' friend.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Oh, and another thing is if you're stung by an Asian Honey Bee, it can produce an allergic reaction in some people, so they're much more dangerous than native bees.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the seminar, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: How will you know if Asian bees have entered Australia?
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: We're looking at the diet of the bird called the Rainbow Bee Eater.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: The bee eater doesn't care what it eats, as long as they're insects.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: But the interesting thing about this bird is that we are able to analyze exactly what it eats, and that's really helpful if we're looking for introduced insects.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: How come?
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: Because insects have their skeletons outside their bodies, so the Bee Eaters digest the meat from the inside. Then they bring up all the indigestible bits of skeleton, and, of course, the wings in a pellet - a small ball of waste material which they cough up.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: That sounds a bit unpleasant, so how do you go about it?
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: In the field, we track down the Bee Eaters and find their favorite feeding spots.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: You know the places where the birds usually feed.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: It's here that we can find the pellets.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: We collect them up and take them back to the laboratory to examine the contents.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: And how do you do that?
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: The pellets are really hard, especially if they've been out in the sun for a few days.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: So, first of all, we treat them by adding water to moisten them.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: And make them softer.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Then we pull them apart under the microscope.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Everything's all scrunched up, but we're looking for wings, so we just pull them all out and straighten them.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Then we identify them to see if we can find any Asian bee wings.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: And how many have you found?
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: So far, our research shows that Asian bees have not entered Australia in any number.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: It's a good result, and much more reliable than trying to find live ones as evidence of introduced insects.
audioscript.speaker: GRANT

audioscript.text: Well, that's fascinating.
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: Thank you Grant for those insights.
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: I hope that you might inspire some of our students here to conduct some similar experiments.
audioscript.speaker: PROFESSOR

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Where in Australia have Asian honey bees been found in the past?
A problem with Asian honey bees is that they
What point is made about Australian bees?
options: Their size stops them from pollinating some flowers
They are sold to customers abroad.

items.title: Grant Freeman says that if Asian honey bees got into Australia,
options: the country's economy would be affected.
they could be used in the study of allergies
certain areas of agriculture would benefit.

items: Birds called Rainbow Bee Eaters eat only <input>, and cough up small bits of skeleton and other products in a pellet.

Researchers go to the locations the bee eaters like to use for <input>. They collect the pellets and take them to a <input> for analysis. Here <input> is used to soften them, and the researchers look for the <input> of Asian bees in the pellets.
The benefit of this research is that the result is more <input> than searching for live Asian bees.
body.title: Looking for Asian honey bees

test_text.title: Research on Public Perception of General Practitioners

audioscript.text: Section 4, you will hear a student called Shona giving a presentation on her research report.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I've been doing some research into what people in Britain think of doctors, the ones who work in general practice - the first call for medical care - and comparing this with the situation in a couple of other countries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I want to talk about the rationale behind what I decided to do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now I had to set up my program of research in three different countries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So I approached postgraduates in my field in overseas departments, contacting them by email to organize things for me at their end.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I thought I would have trouble recruiting help, but in fact everyone was very willing, and sometimes their tutors got involved too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I had to give my helpers clear instructions about what kind of sample population I wanted them to use.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I decided that people under 18 should be excluded, because most of them are students or looking for their first job.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And also I decided at this stage just to focus on men who were in employment, and set up something for people who didn't have jobs, and for employed women later on as a separate investigation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I specifically wanted to do a questionnaire and interviews with a focus group, with the questionnaire rather than limiting it to one specific point.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I wanted to include as much variety as possible.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I know questionnaires are a very controlled way to do things, but I thought I could do taped interviews later on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To counteract the effects of this, and the focus group may also prove useful in future, by targeting subjects I can easily return to.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As the participants tend to be more involved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So I'm just collating the results now, at the moment it looks as if in the UK, despite the fact that newspapers continually report that people are unhappy with medical care.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, it is mainly the third level of care, which takes place in hospitals that they are worried about.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Government reforms have been proposed at all levels, and although their success is not guaranteed, long term hospital care is in fact probably less of an issue than the media would have us believe.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, I've still got quite a bit of data to look at.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Certainly, I will need to do more far-reaching research than I had anticipated, in order to establish if people want extra medical staff invested in the community.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Or if they want care to revert to fewer but larger key medical units.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The solution may well be something that can be easily implemented by those responsible in local government, with central government support of course.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This first stage has proved very valuable though.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I was surprised by how willing most of the subjects were to get involved in the project.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I had expected some unwillingness to answer questions honestly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But I was taken aback and rather concerned that something I thought I'd set up very well didn't necessarily seem that way to everyone in my own department.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I thought you might also be interested in some of the problems I encountered in collecting my data.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There were odd cases that threw me.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the subjects who I had approached while he was out shopping in town decided to pull out when it came to the second round.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was a shame.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As it was someone who I would like to have interviewed more closely.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And one of the first year students I interviewed wanted reassurance that no names would be traceable from the answers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I was so surprised, because they think nothing of telling you about themselves and their opinions in seminar groups.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then one of the people that I worked with got a bit funny.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The questions were quite personal, and one minute he said he'd do it, then the next day he wouldn't.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And in the end, he did do it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's hard not to get angry in that situation, but I tried to keep focused on the overall picture in order to stay calm.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The most bizarre case was a telephone interview I did with a teacher at a university in France.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He answered all my questions in great detail.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But then when I asked how much access he had to dangerous substances, he wouldn't tell me exactly what his work involved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a real eye-opener...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Certainly, I will need to do more far-reaching research than I had anticipated, in order to establish if people want extra medical staff invested in the community.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Or if they want care to revert to fewer but larger key medical units.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The solution may well be something that can be easily implemented by those responsible in local government, with central government support of course.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This first stage has proved very valuable though.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I was surprised by how willing most of the subjects were to get involved in the project.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I had expected some unwillingness to answer questions honestly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But I was taken aback and rather concerned that something I thought I'd set up very well didn't necessarily seem that way to everyone in my own department.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I thought you might also be interested in some of the problems I encountered in collecting my data.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There were odd cases that threw me.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of the subjects who I had approached while he was out shopping in town decided to pull out when it came to the second round.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was a shame.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As it was someone who I would like to have interviewed more closely.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And one of the first year students I interviewed wanted reassurance that no names would be traceable from the answers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I was so surprised, because they think nothing of telling you about themselves and their opinions in seminar groups.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then one of the people that I worked with got a bit funny.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The questions were quite personal, and one minute he said he'd do it, then the next day he wouldn't.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And in the end, he did do it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's hard not to get angry in that situation, but I tried to keep focused on the overall picture in order to stay calm.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The most bizarre case was a telephone interview I did with a teacher at a university in France.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He answered all my questions in great detail.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But then when I asked how much access he had to dangerous substances, he wouldn't tell me exactly what his work involved.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's a real eye-opener...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: In order to set up her research programme, Shona got
options: advice from personal friends in other countries.
help from students in other countries.
information from her tutor's contacts in other countries.

items.title: What types of people were included in the research?
options: young people in their first job

items.title: Shona says that in her questionnaire her aim was
options: to get a wide range of data.

items.title: What do Shona's initial results show about medical services in Britain?
options: Current concerns are misrepresented by the press.
Financial issues are critical to the government
Reforms within hospitals have been unsuccessful.

items.title: Shona needs to do further research in order to
options: present the government with her findings.
decide the level of extra funding needed.
identify the preferences of the public.

items.title: Shona has learnt from the research project that
options: it is important to plan projects carefully.
people do not like answering questions.

text: Which statement applies to each of the following people who were interviewed by Shona?
textReadable: Which statement applies to each of the following people who were interviewed by Shona?

list: refused to tell Shona about their job
kept changing their mind about participating
items: a person interviewed in the street <input>
a tutor in a foreign university    <input>
body.title: People interviewed by Shona
